questions.question_text: Where must a real estate trust account be established in Alabama?
questions.correct_answer: A federally insured institution in Alabama
distractors: Any bank in the United States
A state-chartered bank in any state
A credit union outside Alabama
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires that trust accounts be maintained in a federally insured financial institution located within the state of Alabama. Out-of-state institutions do not satisfy this requirement regardless of their insurance status.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker deposits a buyer's earnest money into the firm's business operating account at a federally insured Alabama bank. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Holding client funds in the operating account instead of a separate trust account
distractors: Failing to title the account with the word 'trust' or 'escrow'
Accepting earnest money without AREC's prior written approval
Using a bank that is federally insured but state-chartered
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires client funds to be held in a separate, federally insured account at an Alabama institution with the qualifying broker as a signatory (Section 34-27-36(b)(8), formerly (a)(8), and Rule 790-X-3-.03(1)). Placing client money in the firm's operating account violates that separate-account requirement and is commingling. There is no requirement that the account be titled 'trust' or 'escrow.'

questions.question_text: A sales associate at a brokerage wants to be the sole authorized signer on the firm's trust account. Is this permitted under Alabama law?
questions.correct_answer: No, the qualifying broker must be a signer
distractors: Yes, if the broker gives written approval
Yes, any licensee in the firm may sign
Only if the firm has fewer than five agents
questions.explanation: Alabama requires the qualifying broker to be an authorized signer on the trust account. A sales associate cannot be the sole signer. The qualifying broker bears ultimate responsibility for the trust account and must maintain signatory authority.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a $200 company check into the firm's trust account to cover the bank's service charges on the account. Has the broker violated Alabama's trust account rules?
questions.correct_answer: No, a small amount of company funds to cover bank charges is permitted
distractors: Yes, this is commingling regardless of the purpose
Yes, unless AREC grants written approval in advance
No, because trust account rules apply only to earnest money
questions.explanation: AREC guidance expressly permits a broker to keep up to $1,000 of company funds in the trust account to cover bank service charges or maintain a minimum balance. A $200 deposit for that purpose falls within the allowance and is not a commingling violation.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker maintains one bank account for both personal savings and client earnest money deposits, carefully tracking each in separate ledgers. An AREC investigator reviews the account. What is the most likely finding?
distractors: No violation if ledgers are accurate
A record-keeping violation only
Permitted if the account is federally insured
questions.explanation: Separate internal ledgers do not cure commingling. Alabama law requires client funds and personal funds to be held in physically separate accounts. Mixing them in one account is a commingling violation regardless of how carefully the broker tracks the balances.

questions.question_text: When must earnest money received with an offer be deposited into the trust account under Alabama law?
questions.correct_answer: Cash immediately; a check within the contract's stated time or when the offer becomes a contract
distractors: Immediately upon receipt, whether cash or check
Within 3 business days of receipt in all cases
Within 7 business days of contract acceptance
questions.explanation: Rule 790-X-3-.03(3) sets a two-track rule: cash must be deposited immediately, while a check is deposited within the time stated in the contract or, if no time is stated, when the offer becomes a contract. The separate duty to act 'immediately upon receipt' belongs to the salesperson, who must pay funds over to the qualifying broker under Rule .03(2).

questions.question_text: A transaction closes on March 1. On March 20, the broker still holds trust funds from that transaction that were due to the parties. Has the broker violated Alabama law?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, funds must be disbursed within 7 business days of consummation
distractors: No, the broker has 30 days after closing
No, funds may be held until all parties agree
Yes, funds had to be disbursed at the closing itself
questions.explanation: Rule 790-X-3-.03, as amended effective April 14, 2025, requires trust funds to be disbursed within 7 business days of consummation of the transaction. Counting business days from a March 1 closing, the deadline falls well before March 20, so continuing to hold the funds is a violation.

questions.question_text: A broker uses $500 from the trust account to pay a personal utility bill, intending to replace it before anyone notices. What consequence does Alabama law impose?
distractors: A warning letter for a first offense
No penalty if funds are replaced promptly
A fine but no license consequences
questions.explanation: Using trust funds for personal purposes is a serious violation subject to disciplinary action by AREC, which may include license suspension or revocation. Replacing the funds does not cure the violation. Trust funds belong to clients and cannot be used for any personal purpose.

questions.question_text: A buyer gives earnest money to a licensee on Friday evening after banking hours. The licensee deposits it Monday morning when the bank opens. Has the licensee violated the deposit requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Likely no, next-business-day handling is acceptable
distractors: Yes, deposit was required Friday evening
Yes, a Saturday deposit was required
No, there is a 5-business-day window
questions.explanation: The deposit rule is two-track: cash goes in immediately, while a check is deposited within the time the contract states or, if none, when the offer becomes a contract (Rule 790-X-3-.03(3)), so a weekend delay in banking the funds is not itself a violation. The licensee's own duty is to pay funds over to the qualifying broker immediately upon receipt, and the next business day is acceptable for funds received after banking hours (Rule .03(2)).

questions.question_text: A broker receives earnest money checks with two offers, and both offers are accepted the same day. Neither contract states a time for depositing the earnest money. One check is deposited promptly; the other is still in a desk drawer a week after its offer became a contract. Which statement is accurate?
questions.correct_answer: Holding the second check a week after contract formation is a violation
distractors: Both checks were handled lawfully
Checks may be held until closing unless a party objects
The broker had 7 business days after acceptance to deposit each check
questions.explanation: Under Rule 790-X-3-.03(3), a check is deposited within the time stated in the contract or, if no time is stated, when the offer becomes a contract. Both offers here were accepted and the contracts are silent on timing, so a check still undeposited a week after contract formation violates the rule.

questions.question_text: How long must an Alabama broker retain trust account records?
distractors: At least 1 year
At least 5 years
At least 7 years
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires brokers to maintain trust account records for a minimum of 3 years. This includes deposit records, disbursement records, and ledgers. Failure to retain records for the full period can result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker hires an office manager to handle all trust account duties and gives her sole authority over deposits and disbursements. An audit reveals a shortage. Who does AREC hold responsible?
distractors: The office manager exclusively
Both equally in all circumstances
Neither if theft is proven
questions.explanation: The qualifying broker is responsible for the trust account; the only relief comes when the parties to the transaction agree in writing to designate someone else to hold the funds (Rule 790-X-3-.03(3)). Delegating day-to-day tasks to an office manager does not transfer that responsibility, and the qualifying broker must also remain an authorized signatory on the account.

questions.question_text: Under what circumstance may AREC audit a broker's trust account?
distractors: Only with a court order
Only if the broker consents in writing
Only after a criminal conviction
questions.explanation: AREC has the authority to audit trust accounts during investigations. No court order or broker consent is required. This investigatory power allows AREC to verify that brokers are properly handling client funds and complying with trust account regulations.

questions.question_text: A qualifying broker signs a written agreement relieving an associate broker of trust account responsibility. The associate broker later mishandles client funds. Who bears responsibility to AREC?
questions.correct_answer: The qualifying broker is still responsible
distractors: The associate broker bears sole liability
Both share equal regulatory liability
Neither if the signed agreement is on file
questions.explanation: Associate licensees never bear the deposit responsibility to begin with — their duty is to pay trust funds over to the qualifying broker immediately upon receipt (Rule 790-X-3-.03(2)). The qualifying broker is relieved of responsibility for holding funds only by the transaction parties' written agreement designating another fund-holder, so a private agreement with an associate broker shifts nothing.

questions.question_text: An AREC investigator requests trust account records from two years ago. The broker says the records were destroyed after one year per office policy. What is the regulatory consequence?
distractors: No consequence if policy was documented
A warning for the first occurrence
AREC must accept the office policy
questions.explanation: Alabama requires trust account records to be retained for at least 3 years. An internal office policy allowing earlier destruction does not override state law. Destroying records after only one year violates the retention requirement and subjects the broker to disciplinary action.
